fuzzy_prd: We need a small, reusable numeric engine that takes a list of closing prices and a look-back window size and produces a full trend or momentum curve — one smoothed value per input price, in order. The engine must support at least four classic signal types: a plain running average, a recency-weighted average that reacts faster to new prices, a linearly tapered average that puts the most emphasis on the latest price, and a bounded momentum oscillator that compares upward pressure to downward pressure on a zero-to-one-hundred scale. All output values must be formatted as fixed four-decimal strings using standard rounding; a mathematically indeterminate result should be represented by a special not-a-number marker rather than crashing. Before a full window of historical data exists, each indicator should degrade gracefully to a shorter window rather than returning nothing. The system must expose a clean command-line adapter that accepts a JSON request on standard input and writes the curve to standard output; if the requested signal type is not recognised, the adapter should emit a two-line neutral error message. The core calculation logic must be fully decoupled from the I/O layer. Agents implementing this should look for the existing indicator hierarchy in the tracker sub-package and the decimal wrapper used throughout the library for exact rounding semantics.

trigger_keywords: what to output when indicator name is not found
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the indicator name is not recognised, emit exactly two lines: first line is 'error=unknown_indicator', second line is 'indicator=<name>' where <name> is the lowercased indicator string from the request.

For SMA, positions before a full timeFrame window exist use a shrinking denominator: at position i (0-based) the average is taken over positions 0..i using divisor (i+1). The effective window is Math.min(timeFrame, index+1). A timeFrame of 1 reproduces the input series exactly.

trigger_keywords: exponential moving average before full window
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For EMA, positions where (index+1 < timeFrame) use the SMA value over the same growing window as the seed. At index==0 when timeFrame > series length, the value equals the first closing price directly. Once (index+1 >= timeFrame) the recurrence applies: prevEMA + multiplier * (currentPrice - prevEMA) where multiplier = 2/(timeFrame+1).

trigger_keywords: weighted moving average before full window
WMA denominator when series shorter than window
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For WMA, when index < timeFrame, weights run from (index+1) down to 1 for positions (0..index), and the divisor is ((index+1)*(index+2))/2. At index==0 the value is the first closing price. When a full window is available, weights run from timeFrame down to 1 and divisor is (timeFrame*(timeFrame+1))/2.
RSI at position 0 (the very first element) is always 0.0000. This is because there are no prior prices to compute any gain or loss from.

trigger_keywords: relative strength index division by zero
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the average loss within the window is zero (no downward price changes), the RSI value is exactly 100 (rendered as '100.0000'). This avoids division by zero and represents maximum upward momentum.

trigger_keywords: how many decimal places in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every output value is formatted as a decimal string rounded half-up to exactly four fractional digits (e.g., '3.3333', '100.0000', '64.7500'). Mathematically undefined results are rendered as the literal string 'NaN'. Formatting uses BigDecimal.setScale(4, RoundingMode.HALF_UP).

trigger_keywords: RSI average gain loss window size
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For RSI, both the average gain and average loss use a divisor of Math.min(timeFrame, index+1), i.e. the number of positions seen so far capped at timeFrame. The cumulated gains/losses within that window are divided by this realTimeFrame to get the averages.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Java package eu.verdelhan.ta4j.indicators.trackers, which contains SMAIndicator.java, EMAIndicator.java, WMAIndicator.java, and RSIIndicator.java — the four core indicator implementations.

context_pointers.vague_reference: decimal wrapper used throughout the library for exact rounding semantics
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The eu.verdelhan.ta4j.Decimal class (Decimal.java), a custom fixed-precision numeric type wrapping BigDecimal that provides methods like plus(), minus(), multipliedBy(), dividedBy(), and a NaN sentinel value used by all indicator calculations.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail is not directly specified in the public contract. Please consult the indicator source files in the trackers sub-package (SMAIndicator, EMAIndicator, WMAIndicator, RSIIndicator) and the Decimal class for precise arithmetic semantics, or refer to the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for expected numeric outputs.

feature1_sma_moving_average.json.description: Compute the simple moving average of a close-price series for a given window length. The output is the indicator curve: one value per input position, in order, each rounded half-up to four decimal places. For positions before a full window of data exists, the average is taken over only the data available so far (a shorter window); once enough data is present, it is the arithmetic mean of the most recent window-length values. A window length of one reproduces the input series.

feature2_ema_moving_average.json.description: Compute the exponential moving average of a close-price series for a given window length. The output is the indicator curve: one value per position, in order, each rounded half-up to four decimal places. Before a full window of data is available, each value equals the simple moving average over the same growing window; once the window is full, each value blends the current price with the previous exponential average using the smoothing weight two divided by (window length plus one). A window length of one reproduces the input series.

feature3_wma_moving_average.json.description: Compute the linearly weighted moving average of a close-price series for a given window length, where within each window the most recent price carries the largest integer weight and the oldest the smallest (weights 1..k summing to k(k+1)/2). The output is the indicator curve: one value per position, in order, each rounded half-up to four decimal places. For positions before a full window exists (including when the window length exceeds the series size), the weighting is applied over only the data available so far.

feature4_rsi_momentum.json.description: Compute the relative strength index of a close-price series for a given window length, using simple averages of upward and downward price changes. The output is the indicator curve: one value per position, in order, each rounded half-up to four decimal places. The first position is always zero. For each later position, the average gain and average loss are the sums of upward (respectively downward) changes within the window divided by the number of positions seen so far (capped at the window length); the value is 100 minus 100/(1+averageGain/averageLoss). When there is no downward change in the window (average loss is zero), the value is exactly 100.
